summary: I was born in a small town in Poland called Goniądz. After World War I, around 1921, I was called to serve in the Polish Army, but my wife urged me to avoid military service and try to go to America instead. Life in Poland was hard, especially with my family struggling, and I sent money back to help them after I left. Unfortunately, I couldn't bring my family to Chicago right away because of my immigration status.

Goniądz is the small town in Poland where I was born and raised. It was there that I got married at nineteen and had two children by the time I was twenty-one. Life in Goniądz was difficult after the war, and it was also where I faced the prospect of being drafted into the Polish Army. My wife wanted me to leave Goniądz and Poland altogether to find a better life in America.

After World War I, I was called to serve in the Polish Army when I was about twenty-one. My wife was very concerned and urged me not to go, encouraging me to try to escape to America instead. I was running away from military service, which made getting a passport impossible, so I had to find other ways to leave Poland. The Polish Army draft was a major reason for my difficult journey out of Europe.

After crossing the border from Poland, I found myself in Germany in the middle of a cold January night. I traveled through Germany with two other men, paying fifteen marks each to a guide who helped us cross. From Germany, we went to Berlin and then took a train to Amsterdam as part of my journey to America. Germany was a key transit point in my escape from Poland.

Berlin was one of the stops on my journey from Poland to America. After crossing into Germany, we took a wagon to Berlin and then caught a train to Amsterdam. It was a brief but important part of the trip, as from Berlin we moved closer to the port where we could catch a boat. Berlin represented a step further away from my old life and closer to my goal of reaching America.

Amsterdam was the city where I boarded a ship heading to Cuba on my way to America. After traveling through Germany and Berlin, I took a train to Amsterdam, Holland, which was the last European stop before crossing the ocean. Amsterdam marked the beginning of the ocean voyage that would eventually bring me closer to Chicago and my family.

I spent about six weeks in Cuba, which was a difficult and tense time. I stayed in a boarding house sharing a room with two other men while I tried to figure out how to get to America. The journey from Cuba to the United States was the most harrowing part, especially the night we crossed the ocean in a tiny overcrowded boat. Cuba was a crucial but challenging stop on my way to Chicago.

Chicago is where I finally arrived and started my new life selling fish, a trade I have continued ever since. I had three brothers and many relatives there, which made it my destination and hope for a better future. When I first got off the train in Chicago and saw my family, I cried like a baby. Although I faced many struggles, including not being able to bring my wife and children at first, Chicago became my home and place of work.

We arrived at Key West, Florida, after a terrifying night and day in a small boat packed with thirteen men. When we landed, the man who smuggled us out of the boat washed our faces with cool water and gave us a little to drink. We were taken to a dirty boarding house near the ocean where we finally got some food and rest, though I couldn’t eat at first. Key West was the first place in the United States where I set foot after my long and dangerous journey.

After arriving in Key West, we took a train to Jacksonville, Florida, where we waited for six hours before continuing on to Chicago. Jacksonville was a brief stop on my journey north, but an important one as part of the route to my final destination. From there, I traveled by train all the way to Chicago to reunite with my family.

The United States was my ultimate goal and new home after escaping Poland and traveling through Europe and Cuba. I entered the U.S. first at Key West, Florida, after a dangerous boat trip, and then traveled north to Chicago where my family lived. Though I faced many challenges, including difficulties with citizenship and bringing my family over, the U.S. gave me the opportunity to build a new life selling fish. It was a place of hope and hardship both.

Because I was smuggled into the United States, I initially could not get citizenship papers and therefore couldn’t bring my wife and children to Chicago. However, a law was passed that allowed people who came to America in 1921 and before to get citizenship papers. This law, which I believe was the Immigration Act of 1924, finally enabled me to become a citizen and bring my wife and daughter to join me in Chicago. It was a turning point that eased some of my troubles.
